input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin system is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes used for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining. Key components include a permanent magnet synchronous motor, gear transmission, cycloidal gear, and Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. Its operational domain is precision manufacturing, where high dynamic control and accuracy are critical, making it suitable for digital twinning due to the need for real-time monitoring and predictive maintenance of mechanical and electrical subsystems.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation leverages Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for hierarchical system abstraction. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs model components, interfaces, and interconnections. Behavior modeling is supported via MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines, MathWorks Simulink for simulation, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Time-series sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol, and live measurements are integrated with structural and behavioral models for operational data.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system architecture integrates SysML for structural modeling, Simulink for dynamic simulations, and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized data exchange, following the Digital Shadow Reference Model to coordinate components and manage resources across the Digital Twin ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol, and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) are implemented for standardized asset representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for structural modeling (e.g., Internal Block Diagrams), with abstractions across requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. Methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) define model types including physics-based analytical models. Behavioral aspects are modeled via SysML state machines and MontiArc Automaton, complemented by simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose code in C++ or Java. Data sources originate from physical assets through automated one-way data flows, with aggregation and abstraction of data traces for specific purposes. Models are integrated across heterogeneous tools (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) and stakeholder perspectives to ensure comprehensive system representation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components consist of a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving a gear transmission with a cycloidal gear mechanism. Actuation is controlled via a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, accepting position, speed, or acceleration inputs. The system's safety cell restricts the maximum working range to [-90°, 90°] for the first axis, with a maximum achievable speed of |q˙ max| = 75 °/s.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive: a motor encoder mounted directly on the motor and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. These encoders reduce backlash and positional uncertainty by providing precise feedback. Time series data from all sensors is accessible for monitoring and analysis, as confirmed in Section IV.A of the source document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Automated one-way data flow between the state of physical objects and digital representation, with time series sensor data streamed via MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol used for streaming time series sensor data from physical sensors to the digital system.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML, AADL, ACME, and MontiArc for architecture description. MBSE methodologies integrate these languages across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer), ensuring interoperability among heterogeneous stakeholders and tools such as Simulink, C++, and Java.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is very accurate regarding the hardware components, correctly identifying the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', the 'permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission', 'cycloidal gear', and the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' controller as described in Section III. However, it loses points for stating the robot is suitable for digital twinning 'due to the need for... predictive maintenance'; the source text justifies the digital shadow for 'enhance robot motion accuracy' and 'identification of joint friction', not explicitly predictive maintenance.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly mentions services such as visualization, identification of friction, and improving motion accuracy. Therefore, the claim that the information is missing is incorrect. Relevant quotes: 'visualization in a dashboard', 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', and 'enhance the robot’s motion accuracy'.

The extraction is highly accurate and well-supported by the text. Section II.A explicitly lists 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE)' methodologies including 'RFLP', 'OOSEM', 'SPES', and 'SMArDT'. It mentions Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) like 'ACME', 'AADL', 'MontiArc', and 'SysML IBDs'. For behavior modeling, it cites 'MontiArc Automaton', 'SysML state machines', and 'MathWorks Simulink', as well as 'general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'. The extraction captures these technical details precisely.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the use of MQTT for streaming time-series sensor data and the integration of live measurements with models. While specific frequencies or latency numbers are not provided in the text, the mention of 'live' data and 'streamed' time-series accurately captures the temporal flow described in the source. Quotes: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.' and 'construct digital shadows that integrate structural and behavioral models, with operational data, and live measurements'.

The source document focuses on a single specific asset (the MABI MAX 100 robot) and specifically its first axis, creating a single digital shadow instance ('FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow'). It does not discuss multiple instances, hierarchical structures, or decentralized architectures. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The extraction accurately identifies SysML for structural modeling ('Fig. 3 shows the robot’s structure as a component & connector SysML model'), Simulink for dynamic simulations ('Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'), and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) ('implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)'). It also correctly references the 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' ('Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model'). The extraction is well-supported and specific to the system described.

The extraction correctly identifies MQTT as the protocol for sensor data streaming ('streamed via MQTT'). However, it states that Asset Administration Shells (AAS) are implemented for 'standardized asset representation,' which is not explicitly found in the text; the text mentions AAS implementation for 'live-data linking.' While the MQTT part is specific and correct, the unsupported detail regarding AAS lowers the score. Quotes: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.' and 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)'.

The description correctly identifies the dual-encoder setup (motor and secondary) and their purpose regarding backlash reduction. It accurately notes the availability of time series data via MQTT. The focus is on applying models to improve accuracy, not on the rigorous quality assurance processes for the models themselves. Thus, the claim that these specific details are not in the document is correct.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The document mentions the 'development phase' in the context of MBSE (Section II.A) and the 'operating time' of the robot (Section I), but it does not provide a detailed description of the Digital Twin's lifecycle phases, transition management between phases, or lifecycle governance. The text focuses on the operational application of a digital shadow for a specific robot axis. Therefore, the claim that this characteristic is 'Not in Document' is correct regarding a comprehensive description.

The paper describes a method for creating a digital shadow (Section II.C and IV.A), involving steps like problem identification, asset analysis, and data identification. However, it does not cover broader Digital Twin engineering processes such as version control, quality assurance practices, or evolution management strategies. The claim that this characteristic is 'Not in Document' is accurate as the text is limited to a specific shadow creation methodology rather than full DT evolution.

The text states the digital shadow's purpose is to 'understand the relations and detection of friction loss in the scope of the robot’s first axis' to improve control accuracy (Section IV.A). It also mentions data visualization in a dashboard. However, it does not describe specific decision support capabilities, analytics processing, user interaction methods, or confidence measures. The claim that this characteristic is 'Not in Document' is accurate because the detailed requirements are not met.

The extraction is highly specific and well-grounded in the source text. Section II.A explicitly mentions 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE)' using 'SysML', 'ACME', 'AADL', and 'MontiArc' as architecture description languages. It details the abstraction levels: 'requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer'. It also references tools like 'Simulink, C++, and Java' for detailed behavior description to integrate heterogeneous stakeholders.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source document focuses entirely on the technical methodology for creating a digital shadow to model robot friction, mentioning tools like 'SysML' and 'Asset Administration Shells'. It does not contain information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection, or consent management. No relevant evidence is present.

While the document mentions a physical safety feature of the robot ('The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range'), it does not address cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or fail-safe mechanisms for the digital twin itself. The text focuses on data linking and 'friction models' rather than security protocols. No relevant evidence regarding the specific security and safety measures defined is present.
